what-is-ai.prompt: What best describes how most of today's AI systems work?

choices.text: They follow a fixed set of hand-written rules for every situation.
choices.feedback: That's classic rule-based software. Modern AI learns patterns from data instead of being told every rule.

choices.text: They learn statistical patterns from large amounts of example data.
choices.feedback: Right — today's AI is pattern-matching at scale, not hand-coded logic.

choices.text: They are conscious and understand meaning the way people do.
choices.feedback: No — they model patterns in data. There's no understanding or awareness behind the output.

what-is-ai.prompt: A model that writes fluent text must therefore understand what it's saying. True?

choices.text: True — fluency requires comprehension.
choices.feedback: Fluency comes from predicting likely next tokens. A model can be confidently, fluently wrong.

choices.text: False — fluency is pattern prediction, not understanding.
choices.feedback: Exactly. Sounding right and being right are different things.

data-and-datasets.prompt: Why does "garbage in, garbage out" matter so much for AI?

choices.text: Models mostly inherit the patterns — and flaws — of the data they're trained on.
choices.feedback: Yes. A model is a mirror of its training data, biases and gaps included.

choices.text: Because more data always makes a model worse.
choices.feedback: Not quantity itself — it's the quality and representativeness of the data that matter.

choices.text: Because models ignore their training data once deployed.
choices.feedback: They don't — everything they 'know' was shaped by that data.

statistics-essentials.prompt: An AI predicts a 70% chance of rain. What does that 70% best represent?

choices.text: It will definitely rain on 70% of the map.
choices.feedback: Probability isn't area — it's a degree of confidence about the outcome.

choices.text: A measure of the model's confidence, not a guarantee.
choices.feedback: Right — it's an estimate of likelihood, and roughly 30% of such days it should be dry.

choices.text: The model is 70% finished computing the answer.
choices.feedback: Probability describes the prediction, not how far along the computation is.

probability-and-uncertainty.prompt: A model gives an answer with high confidence. Can it still be wrong?

choices.text: No — high confidence means it's correct.
choices.feedback: Confidence is the model's own estimate; it can be confidently, fluently wrong.

choices.text: Yes — confidence and correctness are different things.
choices.feedback: Exactly. Whether confidence actually matches accuracy (calibration) is its own hard problem.

correlation-vs-causation.prompt: A model notices ice-cream sales and drownings rise together. What's the safe conclusion?

choices.text: Ice cream causes drowning.
choices.feedback: The classic trap. A shared cause — hot weather — drives both.

choices.text: They're correlated, but one may not cause the other.
choices.feedback: Right — correlation is a pattern; causation needs more than co-occurrence.

choices.text: Drowning causes ice-cream sales.
choices.feedback: Reversing the arrow doesn't fix it — correlation alone can't tell you direction.

patterns-and-predictions.prompt: At its core, generating text from a language model is closest to which task?

choices.text: Looking the answer up in a stored database of facts.
choices.feedback: There's no lookup table — it predicts, it doesn't retrieve (unless you bolt retrieval on).

choices.text: Predicting the next most-likely piece of text, step by step.
choices.feedback: Yes — it's sequential next-token prediction, one piece at a time.

choices.text: Running a formal logical proof to derive the answer.
choices.feedback: It approximates reasoning through patterns; it isn't executing formal logic.

choices.text: A set of learned numbers (parameters) that map inputs to outputs.
choices.feedback: Right — training tunes those weights; the model is those numbers plus its architecture.

choices.text: A physical robot or server.
choices.feedback: Hardware runs the model, but the model itself is the learned parameters.

choices.text: A copy of the internet stored word-for-word.
choices.feedback: It doesn't store text verbatim — it compresses patterns into weights.

bias-in-data.prompt: Where does most harmful bias in AI systems originate?

choices.text: Patterns and imbalances in the training data and its labels.
choices.feedback: Yes — models absorb the skews of their data and how it was labelled.

choices.text: Random electrical noise in the hardware.
choices.feedback: Bias is systematic, not random noise — it traces back to data and design choices.

choices.text: The user's phrasing, and nothing else.
choices.feedback: Phrasing can surface bias, but the bias was learned from data first.

features-and-labels.prompt: In supervised learning, what is a "label"?

choices.text: The correct answer the model is trained to predict.
choices.feedback: Right — features are the inputs, the label is the target answer.

choices.text: The name of the dataset file.
choices.feedback: That's just a filename — a label is the ground-truth output for each example.

choices.text: A tag the model invents entirely on its own.
choices.feedback: Labels are supplied by humans or a process; inventing them is unsupervised territory.

training-vs-testing.prompt: Why hold out a separate test set the model never trains on?

choices.text: To check whether it generalises, not just memorises.
choices.feedback: Exactly — testing on training data flatters the model and hides overfitting.

choices.text: To give the model more data to learn from.
choices.feedback: The test set is deliberately withheld — learning from it defeats its purpose.

choices.text: Because testing is faster than training.
choices.feedback: Speed isn't the point — an honest estimate of real-world performance is.

overfitting-underfitting.prompt: A model scores 99% on its training data but 60% on new data. What's happening?

choices.text: Underfitting — the model is too simple.
choices.feedback: Underfitting scores poorly on both. The big gap points the other way.

choices.text: Overfitting — it memorised the training set instead of generalising.
choices.feedback: Right — a large train/test gap is the signature of overfitting.

choices.text: Perfect generalisation.
choices.feedback: Generalisation means close scores on both; a 39-point gap is the opposite.

neural-networks.prompt: What does a single artificial "neuron" actually do?

choices.text: Stores one fact, like a row in a spreadsheet.
choices.feedback: Neurons don't store facts — they compute a weighted sum and pass it through a function.

choices.text: Takes weighted inputs, sums them, and applies an activation function.
choices.feedback: Yes — stack millions of these and the network learns patterns.

choices.text: Makes the final yes/no decision for the whole network.
choices.feedback: At most that's the output layer's job; one neuron is a tiny building block.

gradient-descent.prompt: Gradient descent improves a model by…

choices.text: randomly guessing weights until one happens to work.
choices.feedback: It's not random — the gradient points toward weights that reduce the error.

choices.text: nudging weights in the direction that lowers the loss, step by step.
choices.feedback: Right — follow the slope downhill on the loss surface, repeatedly.

choices.text: deleting the worst training examples.
choices.feedback: It adjusts the weights, not the dataset.

tokenization.prompt: Before a language model reads your text, what happens first?

choices.text: It's split into tokens — chunks like words or word-pieces — mapped to numbers.
choices.feedback: Yes — models operate on token IDs, not raw letters.

choices.text: It's translated into English.
choices.feedback: There's no translation step — it's tokenized in whatever language it's in.

choices.text: It's checked and corrected for spelling.
choices.feedback: No spell-check stage — the text is simply tokenized.

tokenization.prompt: Why can a common word and a rare word cost different numbers of tokens?

choices.text: Rare or unusual words get split into several sub-word pieces.
choices.feedback: Right — frequent words are often one token; rare ones fragment into many.

choices.text: Longer words are always exactly one token.
choices.feedback: Length doesn't guarantee one token — rare strings split into pieces.

choices.text: Tokens are counted one per character, always.
choices.feedback: Most modern tokenizers use sub-words, not single characters.

choices.text: A list of numbers (a vector) that captures the meaning of a piece of text.
choices.feedback: Yes — similar meanings land near each other in that vector space.

choices.text: A compressed image file.
choices.feedback: Embeddings are numeric vectors for meaning, not an image format.

choices.text: The raw text, stored exactly as written.
choices.feedback: It's a learned numeric representation, not the original text.

embeddings.prompt: In embedding space, "king" and "queen" end up close together because…

choices.text: they're spelled similarly.
choices.feedback: Spelling isn't it — 'king' and 'kindle' look alike but mean different things.

choices.text: they appear in similar contexts, so they get similar vectors.
choices.feedback: Right — meaning comes from usage patterns, captured as nearby vectors.

choices.text: they have the same number of letters.
choices.feedback: Letter count has nothing to do with embeddings.

transformers.prompt: What key idea made transformers so effective for language?

choices.text: An attention mechanism that weighs the relationships between all tokens.
choices.feedback: Yes — attention lets each token look at every other token in context.

choices.text: Reading text strictly one word at a time, like older RNNs.
choices.feedback: That's the older approach transformers replaced; attention sees the whole context at once.

choices.text: Storing the entire internet to look answers up.
choices.feedback: No lookup store — they learn weighted patterns through attention.

transformers.prompt: In a transformer, "attention" lets a token…

choices.text: decide which other tokens matter most for interpreting it.
choices.feedback: Right — attention assigns learned weights to other tokens' relevance.

choices.text: pay exactly equal attention to every token, always.
choices.feedback: The whole point is unequal, learned weighting — not uniform attention.

choices.text: ignore all of the other tokens.
choices.feedback: The opposite — attention is about relating tokens to each other.

attention.prompt: In "The trophy didn't fit in the suitcase because it was too big", attention helps the model figure out…

choices.text: what "it" refers to, by weighing the surrounding words.
choices.feedback: Exactly — attention links 'it' to 'trophy' using the context.

choices.text: the correct spelling of "suitcase".
choices.feedback: Attention is about relationships in meaning, not spelling.

choices.text: nothing — pronouns are skipped entirely.
choices.feedback: Resolving references like this is precisely what attention is good at.

choices.text: The maximum amount of text it can consider at once.
choices.feedback: Right — beyond that limit, earlier content falls out of view.

choices.text: The size of your browser window.
choices.feedback: It's a token limit inside the model, not anything on your screen.

choices.text: How long the model spent training.
choices.feedback: That's training time; the context window is about input length at inference.

temperature-sampling.prompt: Raising the "temperature" when generating text does what?

choices.text: Makes the output more random and varied.
choices.feedback: Yes — higher temperature flattens the odds, so less-likely tokens appear more often.

choices.text: Makes the hardware physically run hotter.
choices.feedback: It's a sampling parameter, not hardware heat.

choices.text: Guarantees more factual answers.
choices.feedback: Higher temperature usually means less predictable output, not more accurate.

prompt-engineering.prompt: Why does giving a model an example or two in the prompt often help?

choices.text: It demonstrates the format and task, steering the next prediction.
choices.feedback: Right — in-context examples shape what the model produces next.

choices.text: It permanently retrains the model's weights.
choices.feedback: Prompting doesn't change weights — the effect lasts only for that request.

choices.text: It makes the model run faster.
choices.feedback: Examples affect quality and format, not speed — they can even cost more tokens.

rag.prompt: What problem does Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) mainly solve?

choices.text: Giving the model relevant, up-to-date facts it wasn't trained on.
choices.feedback: Yes — retrieve documents, then let the model answer grounded in them.

choices.text: Making the model retrain itself overnight.
choices.feedback: RAG adds context at query time; it doesn't retrain anything.

choices.text: Removing the need for a model at all.
choices.feedback: RAG feeds a model — it doesn't replace one.

rag.prompt: In RAG, where do the retrieved documents go?

choices.text: Into the model's prompt/context for that single answer.
choices.feedback: Right — they're injected as context, not baked into the weights.

choices.text: Permanently into the model's parameters.
choices.feedback: Nothing is written to the weights — retrieval happens per request.

choices.text: They replace the user's question.
choices.feedback: They supplement the question as supporting context, not replace it.

hallucination.prompt: Why do language models sometimes "hallucinate" confident but false statements?

choices.text: They generate plausible-sounding text, and plausible isn't always true.
choices.feedback: Exactly — they optimise for likely continuations, not verified facts.

choices.text: Someone manually typed the wrong answer in.
choices.feedback: No human typed it — the model produced a fluent guess.

choices.text: They always know when they're unsure and say so.
choices.feedback: They often don't — confidence and correctness can come apart.

loss-functions.prompt: What does a loss function measure during training?

choices.text: How far the model's predictions are from the right answers.
choices.feedback: Right — training works to make that number smaller.

choices.text: How fast the model runs.
choices.feedback: That's speed/latency, not loss. Loss is about prediction error.

choices.text: How much memory the model uses.
choices.feedback: That's resource usage — loss measures how wrong the predictions are.

regression-classification.prompt: What's the core difference between regression and classification?

choices.text: Regression predicts a number; classification predicts a category.
choices.feedback: Exactly — a price vs a label like spam/not-spam.

choices.text: Regression is for images, classification for text.
choices.feedback: Neither is tied to a data type — the difference is number vs category.

choices.text: They're two names for the same thing.
choices.feedback: They're distinct tasks: continuous values vs discrete classes.

clustering.prompt: Clustering is an example of which kind of learning?

choices.text: Unsupervised — it groups data without labels.
choices.feedback: Right — it finds structure on its own, no answer key.

choices.text: Supervised — it needs labelled examples.
choices.feedback: No labels required — that's what makes clustering unsupervised.

choices.text: Reinforcement — it learns from rewards.
choices.feedback: No reward signal here; clustering just groups similar points.

evaluation-metrics.prompt: Why isn't raw accuracy always a good metric?

choices.text: On imbalanced data, always guessing the majority class can still score high.
choices.feedback: Exactly — 99% accuracy is meaningless if 99% of cases are one class.

choices.text: Accuracy is always the single best metric.
choices.feedback: It can hide real failure modes — that's the whole point of the question.

choices.text: Accuracy can't be computed for classifiers.
choices.feedback: It can — it's just not always the most informative measure.

backpropagation.prompt: What does backpropagation do during training?

choices.text: Sends the error backward through the network to update each weight.
choices.feedback: Right — it figures out how much each weight contributed to the error.

choices.text: Generates new training data.
choices.feedback: It adjusts weights using existing data; it doesn't create data.

choices.text: Chooses which examples to train on.
choices.feedback: That's data selection — backprop is about propagating the error to weights.

gpus-and-compute.prompt: Why are GPUs central to modern AI?

choices.text: They do the massively parallel matrix math training needs, far faster than CPUs.
choices.feedback: Right — neural nets are mostly matrix multiplications, which GPUs excel at.

choices.text: They store the training data.
choices.feedback: Storage is disks; GPUs do the heavy parallel computation.

choices.text: They write the model's code.
choices.feedback: They run the math, they don't author code.

diffusion.prompt: How does a diffusion model generate an image?

choices.text: It starts from noise and removes it step by step toward a coherent image.
choices.feedback: Right — it learns to reverse a noising process, denoising into an image.

choices.text: It pastes together cropped pieces of training images.
choices.feedback: No collage — it denoises from scratch, it doesn't copy-paste.

choices.text: It looks the image up in a stored database.
choices.feedback: There's no lookup — it generates pixels by iterative denoising.

mixture-of-experts.prompt: What does a mixture-of-experts (MoE) model do?

choices.text: Routes each input to a few specialised sub-networks instead of using the whole model.
choices.feedback: Right — only a fraction of the parameters fire per token, saving compute.

choices.text: Averages the answers of many separate full models.
choices.feedback: That's ensembling — MoE routes within one model to chosen experts.

choices.text: Asks human experts to label the data.
choices.feedback: The 'experts' are sub-networks, not people.

choices.text: It works across more than one kind of input — e.g. text and images together.
choices.feedback: Right — multiple modalities like text, images, audio in one model.

choices.text: It runs in multiple languages.
choices.feedback: Many languages is still one modality (text). Multimodal spans types of input.

choices.text: It runs multiple copies at once.
choices.feedback: That's scaling, not modality — multimodal is about kinds of input.

choices.text: connect images and text — e.g. describe a photo or answer questions about it.
choices.feedback: Right — it ties what it 'sees' to language.

choices.text: only process text, never images.
choices.feedback: The 'vision' half means it handles images too.

choices.text: only generate images, never read them.
choices.feedback: It can interpret images, not just make them.

choices.text: Training a smaller "student" model to mimic a larger "teacher" model.
choices.feedback: Right — you get most of the quality in a cheaper, faster model.

choices.text: Removing water from the training data.
choices.feedback: It's a model-compression idea, nothing to do with literal distilling.

choices.text: Deleting the worst-performing layers.
choices.feedback: That's pruning — distillation trains a student to copy a teacher.

dpo.prompt: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) tunes a model using…

choices.text: pairs of preferred vs rejected answers, optimised directly without a separate reward model.
choices.feedback: Right — it skips RLHF's reward-model step and learns straight from preferences.

choices.text: a hand-written rulebook of correct answers.
choices.feedback: It learns from comparative preferences, not a fixed rulebook.

choices.text: more raw pretraining text.
choices.feedback: That's pretraining — DPO is preference alignment.

choices.text: further training a pretrained model on a narrower dataset to specialise it.
choices.feedback: Right — you start from a general model and adapt its weights.

choices.text: writing a longer, more detailed prompt.
choices.feedback: That's prompting — fine-tuning actually updates the weights.

choices.text: running the model on a faster GPU.
choices.feedback: Hardware doesn't specialise a model; training on new data does.

choices.text: Quality degrades when models are trained repeatedly on AI-generated data.
choices.feedback: Right — errors compound as models learn from other models' output.

choices.text: The training server physically crashes.
choices.feedback: It's a data-quality spiral, not a hardware failure.

choices.text: The model forgets its own name.
choices.feedback: It's about degrading output quality across generations, not amnesia.

quantization.prompt: What does quantization do to a model?

choices.text: Stores its weights at lower numerical precision to shrink size and speed it up.
choices.feedback: Right — e.g. 16-bit down to 4-bit, trading a little accuracy for efficiency.

choices.text: Adds more parameters to boost accuracy.
choices.feedback: The opposite — it compresses existing weights, it doesn't add any.

choices.text: Encrypts the weights for security.
choices.feedback: It reduces precision, not for secrecy but for size and speed.

rlhf.prompt: What does RLHF (reinforcement learning from human feedback) do?

choices.text: Tunes a model toward responses humans rated as better.
choices.feedback: Right — human preferences train a reward signal the model optimises for.

choices.text: Replaces all training data with human-written text.
choices.feedback: It uses preference ratings to fine-tune, not a wholesale data swap.

choices.text: Lets the model browse the internet live.
choices.feedback: That's retrieval/tools — RLHF is about aligning to human preferences.

choices.text: How performance improves predictably as model size, data, and compute grow.
choices.feedback: Right — smooth, roughly power-law gains as you scale the inputs.

choices.text: How to scale a website's traffic.
choices.feedback: Different 'scaling' — these laws are about model performance vs resources.

choices.text: Legal limits on how large a model may be.
choices.feedback: They're empirical performance trends, not regulations.

choices.text: data generated by a model or simulation rather than collected from the real world.
choices.feedback: Right — useful to fill gaps, but it can amplify a model's own biases.

choices.text: data stored in a fake database.
choices.feedback: It's about how the data was produced, not where it's stored.

choices.text: data that has been deleted.
choices.feedback: Synthetic means generated, not removed.

training.prompt: What happens during "training" of a model?

choices.text: Its weights are adjusted over many examples to reduce prediction error.
choices.feedback: Right — repeated passes nudge the parameters toward better predictions.

choices.text: It memorises a fixed script of answers.
choices.feedback: It learns general patterns in weights, not a lookup script.

choices.text: It downloads a finished model from the internet.
choices.feedback: That's loading a model — training is the process that creates one.

choices.text: Asking the model to reason step by step before giving its answer.
choices.feedback: Right — laying out intermediate steps often improves hard answers.

choices.text: Chaining several separate models into a pipeline.
choices.feedback: That's a multi-model pipeline — chain-of-thought is one model reasoning aloud.

choices.text: Linking the model directly to a database.
choices.feedback: No database — it's about the model showing its working.

choices.text: output a structured request to run a tool or API, then use the result.
choices.feedback: Right — the model picks the function and arguments; your code runs it.

choices.text: rewrite its own source code.
choices.feedback: It calls external tools, it doesn't edit itself.

choices.text: train itself on new data.
choices.feedback: Calling a function isn't training — no weights change.

in-context-learning.prompt: In-context learning is when a model…

choices.text: picks up a task from examples in the prompt, with no weight updates.
choices.feedback: Right — it 'learns' for that request only; nothing is saved to the weights.

choices.text: permanently learns from every conversation.
choices.feedback: No — the effect lasts only within the prompt's context.

choices.text: is retrained overnight on user chats.
choices.feedback: That would be fine-tuning; in-context learning changes nothing permanently.

kv-cache.prompt: What problem does the KV cache solve?

choices.text: It avoids recomputing earlier tokens' attention, speeding up generation.
choices.feedback: Right — past keys/values are stored so each new token is cheaper.

choices.text: It stores the user's password.
choices.feedback: Nothing to do with credentials — it caches attention state.

choices.text: It permanently saves the conversation to disk.
choices.feedback: It's a short-lived in-memory speedup, not persistent storage.

choices.text: It spends extra compute thinking through steps before answering.
choices.feedback: Right — more deliberate intermediate reasoning, often better on hard problems.

choices.text: It never makes mistakes.
choices.feedback: It still can — it just reasons more before answering.

choices.text: It has literally read every book ever written.
choices.feedback: Its edge is how it processes, not impossibly complete knowledge.

structured-output.prompt: Why ask a model for structured output like JSON?

choices.text: So its answer can be reliably parsed and used by other software.
choices.feedback: Right — predictable structure lets code consume the output safely.

choices.text: Because models can't produce plain prose.
choices.feedback: They can — structure is for machine-readability, not a limitation.

choices.text: To make the model run faster.
choices.feedback: It's about usable format, not speed.

agentic-memory.prompt: What is "memory" for an AI agent?

choices.text: A way to store and recall information across steps or sessions, beyond the context window.
choices.feedback: Right — it lets an agent remember past actions and facts over time.

choices.text: The RAM installed in the server.
choices.feedback: Not hardware memory — it's persisted task state the agent can recall.

choices.text: The model's original training data.
choices.feedback: Training data is baked in; agent memory is what it accrues while working.

choices.text: It can take actions — using tools in a loop toward a goal — not just produce text.
choices.feedback: Right — perceive, decide, act, repeat, rather than a single reply.

choices.text: It runs with no model behind it.
choices.feedback: An agent is built on a model plus tools and a loop.

choices.text: It's a human assistant.
choices.feedback: Here 'agent' means the autonomous software, not a person.

choices.text: Systematic tests that measure how well a model performs a task.
choices.feedback: Right — they turn 'seems good' into something measurable and repeatable.

choices.text: The model's internal monologue.
choices.feedback: That's reasoning traces — evals are external measurements.

choices.text: A kind of training data.
choices.feedback: Evals assess a model; they aren't what it trains on.

mcp.prompt: What does the Model Context Protocol (MCP) standardise?

choices.text: How AI apps connect to external tools and data sources.
choices.feedback: Right — a common plug so models and tools interoperate.

choices.text: How models are physically manufactured.
choices.feedback: It's a software interface, not hardware.

choices.text: The pricing of API calls.
choices.feedback: It's about connectivity, not billing.

choices.text: A short document describing a model's intended use, limits, and risks.
choices.feedback: Right — like a nutrition label for a model.

choices.text: A collectible trading card with the model's stats.
choices.feedback: It's documentation for responsible use, not a collectible.

choices.text: The invoice for using the model.
choices.feedback: It's about transparency, not billing.

choices.text: several AI agents coordinating, often with different roles, on a task.
choices.feedback: Right — e.g. a planner, a researcher, and a critic working together.

choices.text: one agent simply run several times for speed.
choices.feedback: That's parallelism — multi-agent means distinct cooperating agents.

choices.text: a single model with extra memory.
choices.feedback: More memory isn't multiple agents.

retrieval-rerank.prompt: In retrieval, what does a reranker do?

choices.text: Reorders the first-pass results so the most relevant rise to the top.
choices.feedback: Right — a cheap search casts a wide net; the reranker sharpens the order.

choices.text: Deletes the user's query.
choices.feedback: It refines results for the query, it doesn't discard it.

choices.text: Writes the final answer itself.
choices.feedback: That's the generator's job — the reranker just orders retrieved items.

choices.text: Getting a model's goals and behaviour to match human intentions and values.
choices.feedback: Right — making capable systems also do what we actually want.

choices.text: Lining up the text neatly on screen.
choices.feedback: Not layout — it's about goals matching human intent.

choices.text: Synchronising servers in a data centre.
choices.feedback: That's infrastructure; alignment is about behaviour and values.

constitutional-ai.prompt: Constitutional AI trains a model to…

choices.text: critique and revise its own answers against a set of written principles.
choices.feedback: Right — the 'constitution' guides self-correction, reducing human labelling.

choices.text: memorise a country's actual constitution.
choices.feedback: The 'constitution' is a set of behavioural principles, not a legal text.

choices.text: follow no rules at all.
choices.feedback: The opposite — it's explicitly governed by stated principles.

jailbreaks.prompt: What is a "jailbreak" of an AI model?

choices.text: A prompt crafted to get the model to bypass its safety rules.
choices.feedback: Right — tricking the model into doing what it's meant to refuse.

choices.text: Installing the model on a phone.
choices.feedback: Nothing to do with devices — it's about defeating guardrails.

choices.text: Making the model run faster.
choices.feedback: It's a safety bypass, not a performance trick.

prompt-injection.prompt: Prompt injection is an attack where…

choices.text: hidden instructions in content trick the model into doing something unintended.
choices.feedback: Right — e.g. a web page telling the model to ignore its real instructions.

choices.text: too many prompts crash the server.
choices.feedback: That's a load problem — injection is about smuggled instructions.

choices.text: the user simply types very fast.
choices.feedback: Speed is irrelevant; it's about malicious instructions in the input.

watermarking.prompt: What is the goal of watermarking AI output?

choices.text: Embedding a detectable signal so AI-generated content can be identified later.
choices.feedback: Right — a statistical fingerprint, usually invisible to the reader.

choices.text: Adding a visible company logo to every answer.
choices.feedback: Watermarks are typically hidden signals, not visible branding.

choices.text: Protecting the model from computer viruses.
choices.feedback: It's about provenance of output, not security.
